profile.name: Leonard Stringfield
profile.died: December 18, 1994 - Cincinnati, Ohio (died on his 74th birthday)
roles: Director, Civilian Research, Interplanetary Flying Objects (CRIFO, 1954-1957)
Author - UFO Crash/Retrievals Status Reports I-VII (1978-1994)
Board Member, National Investigations Committee on Aerial Phenomena (NICAP)
U.S. Air Force Consultant on UFO Reports (1950s)
Public Relations Director, DuBois Chemicals, Cincinnati
organizations: National Investigations Committee on Aerial Phenomena (NICAP)
profile.summary: Leonard Stringfield was a pioneering UAP researcher who spent 16 years documenting alleged crash retrieval cases through a series of seven self-published 'Status Reports' (1978-1994), ultimately compiling testimony from over 100 sources including military personnel, medical professionals, and government contractors who claimed firsthand knowledge of recovered craft and bodies. His systematic methodology of protecting source anonymity while cataloging testimony established the template for crash retrieval research and influenced every subsequent researcher in this field.
early_career: Served as a U.S. Army Air Corps enlisted man during World War II (1942-1945)
Had a personal UFO sighting in August 1945 while aboard a military transport near Japan - three bright objects caused engine failure before departing
Founded CRIFO (Civilian Research, Interplanetary Flying Objects) in Cincinnati in 1954, which grew to become one of the largest civilian UFO organizations of the 1950s
Published the CRIFO newsletter 'Orbit', which reached a circulation of over 2,000 by 1955
Was appointed as a UFO reporting consultant to the U.S. Air Defense Command's Filter Center in Cincinnati in the mid-1950s

key_events.event: While aboard a military C-46 transport near Japan, experienced a personal UAP encounter: three bright objects appeared, the aircraft's engines lost power, and the objects departed at high speed before the plane was able to land safely. The experience shaped his lifelong research focus.

Founded CRIFO (Civilian Research, Interplanetary Flying Objects) in Cincinnati, Ohio. The organization became one of the largest civilian UAP investigation groups in the U.S., publishing the newsletter 'Orbit' and building a national contact network.
Published 'Inside Saucer Post 3-0 Blue,' his first book documenting the CRIFO years and early witness cases.

Appointed as a civilian UFO reporting consultant to the U.S. Air Defense Command's Filter Center in Cincinnati - one of the few civilian researchers to hold a formal advisory role with the U.S. military on UAP matters.
Published 'Situation Red: The UFO Siege,' a comprehensive review of UAP evidence that reached mainstream audiences and set the stage for his crash retrieval research.

Published 'UFO Crash/Retrievals: Amassing the Evidence - Status Report I,' initiating his most significant research program. The report compiled testimony from anonymous military and medical sources claiming direct knowledge of crash recovery operations and recovered biological specimens.

Published Status Report II, expanding the source pool and establishing his methodology of protecting witness anonymity while providing enough detail for independent researchers to verify categories of claims.

Published Status Report III, by which point Stringfield had compiled testimony from several dozen sources. He presented at MUFON and CUFOS symposia, bringing crash retrieval research into mainstream UAP conference circuits.
Published Status Reports IV and V, significantly expanding the documented case base and including testimony from medical personnel describing recovered biological entities.
Published Status Report VI. By this point Stringfield had become the central clearinghouse for crash retrieval testimony, with researchers from around the world forwarding source contacts to him.

Published Status Report VII - his final report - in the months before his death. The complete seven-report series documents testimony from over 100 sources and remains the foundational archive of alleged crash retrieval witness accounts.

Died on his 74th birthday in Cincinnati, Ohio. Stanton Friedman, who had collaborated with Stringfield on crash retrieval research for years, described him as 'the godfather of crash retrieval research.'

claims.claim: That U.S. military and government agencies have recovered multiple non-human craft and biological entities since at least the late 1940s, and that this information has been systematically concealed from the public.
claims.notes: Stringfield himself never claimed firsthand knowledge; his role was as a compiler and documenter of others' testimony. The claim rests on the aggregate of over 100 anonymous sources across his seven Status Reports. No individual source has been publicly named or independently verified, though researchers including Stanton Friedman have independently corroborated some source categories.

claims.claim: Documented testimony from multiple medical personnel - including alleged surgeons and pathologists with claimed military clearances - describing performing autopsies on non-human biological entities with large heads, large dark eyes, small features, and grayish skin.
claims.notes: These accounts, appearing primarily in Status Reports III-VI, are among the most specific in crash retrieval literature. All sources remained anonymous at their own request. The physical descriptions provided across independent sources showed notable consistency - a pattern Stringfield argued could not be explained by cultural contamination given the pre-internet, pre-Roswell-media-saturation timeframe of many accounts.

credibility.assessment: Stringfield's credibility rests primarily on his methodology rather than his claims. As a researcher who never asserted firsthand knowledge, he operated as a documentarian and archivist - a role that invited scrutiny of his source vetting rather than his personal reliability. His CRIFO work, his Air Defense Command consulting role, and his formal research presentations gave him institutional standing unusual for UAP investigators of his era. The significant limitation is the anonymity of virtually all his sources: the 100+ witnesses in his Status Reports cannot be independently contacted or verified, making the corpus compelling but unverifiable.
supporting: Operated as a documentarian rather than a firsthand witness - his claims are sourced, not personal
His CRIFO organization was formally recognized by the U.S. Air Defense Command, which appointed him as a civilian UFO reporting consultant in the 1950s
Compiled testimony over 16 years from over 100 separate sources - a scope that represents genuine long-term research commitment
His methodology of recording source anonymity while documenting technical detail categories set the standard for subsequent crash retrieval research
Contemporaries including Stanton Friedman, who also independently sourced crash retrieval witnesses, described his work as foundational and methodologically careful
contradicting: Virtually all 100+ sources in his Status Reports remained anonymous - there is no mechanism for independent verification
The anonymous testimony format makes it impossible to distinguish genuine witnesses from fabricators, wishful thinkers, or misinformation sources
Stringfield had no security clearance and no access to classified records - he could not verify his sources' claimed military or government roles
The consistency across accounts that Stringfield cited as evidence could alternatively reflect contamination by shared cultural narratives circulating in UAP research communities
None of the physical evidence - craft materials, biological specimens - described by his sources has ever been produced for examination

associated_people.name: Stanton Friedman
associated_people.relationship: Friedman and Stringfield were the two central figures in crash retrieval research from the late 1970s onward, frequently exchanging sources and corroborating findings. Friedman described Stringfield as 'the godfather of crash retrieval research' and continued his work after Stringfield's death.

associated_people.name: Maj. Donald Keyhoe
associated_people.relationship: Stringfield served on NICAP's board under Keyhoe's directorship. Their institutional relationship positioned Stringfield as a key researcher within the most significant civilian UAP organization of the 1950s-60s.

associated_people.name: Richard Dolan
associated_people.relationship: Dolan's historical research on UAP secrecy drew extensively on Stringfield's Status Reports as primary source material. Dolan has consistently cited Stringfield as the foundational figure in documenting crash retrieval claims.

disclosures.title: Inside Saucer Post 3-0 Blue
disclosures.notes: First book documenting the CRIFO years, Stringfield's 1945 personal sighting, and early civilian UAP investigation network.

disclosures.title: Situation Red: The UFO Siege
disclosures.notes: Mainstream-published overview of UAP evidence, setting the stage for his crash retrieval research program.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: Amassing the Evidence - Status Report I
disclosures.notes: First of seven Status Reports; initiated the systematic documentation of crash retrieval testimony from anonymous military and medical sources.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: The Inner Sanctum - Status Report II
disclosures.notes: Expanded source pool; strengthened anonymous testimony methodology.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: Retrievals of the Third Kind - Status Report III
disclosures.notes: Included testimony from alleged medical personnel describing biological entities.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: Status Reports IV and V
disclosures.notes: Expanded biological entity testimony and broadened the documented source geography internationally.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: Status Report VI
disclosures.notes: Continued expansion of the case archive; Stringfield by this point served as a central clearinghouse for international crash retrieval testimony.

disclosures.title: UFO Crash/Retrievals: Search for Proof in a Hall of Mirrors - Status Report VII
disclosures.notes: Final report; completed months before his death. The seven-report series collectively documents testimony from over 100 sources.

sources.title: Leonard Stringfield - UFO Crash/Retrievals Status Reports I-VII (1978-1994)
sources.notes: Primary source; the complete seven-volume archive of Stringfield's crash retrieval research

sources.title: Situation Red: The UFO Siege - Leonard Stringfield (Doubleday, 1977)
sources.notes: Mainstream-published overview; primary biographical and career context source

sources.title: Stanton Friedman - 'Leonard Stringfield: The Godfather of Crash Retrieval Research'
sources.notes: Friedman's assessment of Stringfield's career and methodology; published after Stringfield's death

sources.title: Richard Dolan - UFOs and the National Security State Vol. 1 (2002)
sources.notes: Dolan's historical work draws extensively on Stringfield's Status Reports as primary source material

career_connections.relationship: Stringfield served on NICAP's board under Keyhoe's directorship, embedding himself within the most significant civilian UAP organization of the 1950s.
Friedman and Stringfield were the two central crash retrieval researchers of the era, frequently exchanging sources; Friedman continued Stringfield's work after his death.
Dolan's historical research drew extensively on Stringfield's Status Reports as foundational primary source material.
